text: Thanks for joining us on This is My Architecture. My name is Wal, I'm from AWS and I'm joined here
today by Ili from Onaz. Welcome Ili. Thank you for having me. So Ili, tell us a bit more about
Onaz and what you guys do. Sure. So Onaz is a luxury e-commerce company, a part of Altair.
We focus on delivering great customer experience with a high degree of personalization.
Great. I see a really interesting architecture here and you used it to solve an interesting
challenge. Can you tell us a bit more about the challenge that you solved with this architecture?
Sure. So in order to provide personalization for our customers, we've built an item-based
recommender system that uses clickstream data. Yep. And I see here that you have state machines
and you have a few machine learning services. What do you do with state machines here?
Sure. So my state machine consists of three steps. It's a whole workflow that manages data pulling,
training, training and serving my model. And this whole workflow is triggered periodically by an event
bridge rule so that the model is always refreshed. Great. So I'm assuming that the three stages are
a projection for the machine learning lifecycle. So the first one is, can you tell us a bit more about
the first stage? Sure. So the first stage is the training step. So in this step, we have a Lambda
function that's invoked and this is going to query Athena, which is going to pull browsing data of our
customers from the lake. It's going to process it, remove outliers and so on. And it's going to push the
results into an S3 bucket that's going to be used in production. Great. So in the first stage, you pre-process
your data, prepare it for the next stage, which is training a machine learning model. So what happened
here? What happens in the second stage? Sure. So when this step is done, a SageMaker training job is
triggered. And this is going to read from this S3 bucket, the browsing data, so that we train a word-to-vac
algorithm using the Blazing Text container by SageMaker. Great. So why did you decide to use SageMaker
instead of something like EC2, for example? So essentially, SageMaker streamlines the whole
model training and model deployment workflow and has capabilities to monitor the endpoint behavior
and so on. And why Blazing Text as an algorithm? Yeah, sure. So I used the Blazing Text container by
SageMaker, which had the word-to-vac algorithm. And word-to-vac was used here to pull item representations as
vectors of all of our items or our catalog so that we can provide similar items in terms of vector
proximity. Great. And then you finish training and you save your model in an O3 bucket. What happens
off of that? So after that, the last step is a SageMaker deployment job in which SageMaker takes
care of configuring an endpoint and then deploying it into production. Great. And I see a browser here.
And this is where the user is interacting with this SageMaker model. So what happens from the browser
all the way back to the model? Sure. So when the endpoint is deployed, as users browse through the
platform of Funas, they will go through product pages. And when they go through a certain product
pages, a request is triggered and calls API Gateway. And then what happens, API Gateway is going to invoke
a Lambda function that's going to request a recommendation from the endpoint and get the
recommended items back, which is then going to be served into the front end as recommendations for the user.
So since SageMaker is giving you an endpoint, why do you use API Gateway here? Sure. So API Gateway
is going to streamline both the authentication and routing all the requests into several endpoints by
SageMaker. So essentially, it makes it easier for me to both handle security and manage the different
APIs that I have under the hood. This is great. I also understand that testing and
evaluating recommendation engine or recommendation models can be a bit challenging. So how did you go about testing your model?
So what we've done is that we collaborated with the CRM team and we launched an AB test that had a duration of about two weeks.
And we had two groups, a control group which was served recommendations based on the most popular items
and the test group in which we served recommendations that were based on this Word2Vec algorithm.
And we've observed at the end of that duration, an uplift of 0.6% of the conversion rate between the two groups.
So thanks for sharing this. What's next in this project?
Sure. So the model that we've deployed is going to have a hard time to handle the call start problem.
So new items to our catalog. So what we're planning to do is we're planning to enrich it by including
a recommender system that's based on images and another one that's based on product description
and make a hybrid workflow that's going to leverage all of these three data sets.
This has been fascinating. Thanks for joining us today and thanks for sharing your architecture.
Thank you for giving me the opportunity to share my work.
Wonderful. Thanks for joining us and this is my architecture. We hope you enjoyed it.
Thank you.
